Market Mythbusting with Gargi Chaudhuri: Is Investing Expensive?
summary: Gargi Chaudhuri argues investing isn't expensive — you can start with whatever you can comfortably afford, since ETFs provide diversified exposure at low cost. Cautions against frequent buy/sell churn (eats into returns via the difficulty of market timing) and positions ETFs as both low-fee and tax-efficient, making them well-suited to long-term investors.

title: ETFs: An Innovation of Index Investing
summary: Introduces what an index is — a rules-based measurement that tracks the performance of a group of securities — and walks through how index ETFs replicate that exposure at low cost relative to actively managed funds. Covers major indexes (Dow Jones, S&P 500, NASDAQ 100, MSCI EAFE, Bloomberg US Aggregate Bond) and surveys specific iShares ETFs across size, asset class, dividend, and thematic strategies.

title: Thinking Globally with ETFs
summary: An iShares presentation on home-country bias. The average US investor holds ~26% international stocks vs ~39% in the MSCI ACWI benchmark. US stocks ranked 36th of 47 in a recent global index, while rest-of-world is ~85% of GDP and ~96% of population. Surveys global innovations (BioNTech mRNA, DiDi 60M rides/day vs Uber 3M) and positions iShares international ETFs — IXUS, IEFA, IEMG, ESGD/ESGE — to address the bias.

title: Income investing with ETFs
summary: iShares presentation on income investing with ETFs in a high-inflation environment. CPI ran ~8% YoY (fastest in 40 years; long-run US avg ~2% since 1871) while Fed funds rate was just lifting off zero. Walks through income tools — bonds (interest-rate and credit risks), dividend-paying stocks, and dividend Growers that historically outperformed through inflation. Surveys iShares income ETFs: USIG, USHY, GOVT, TIP, STIP, HDV, and IYLD multi-asset.

title: Sustainability   A new standard for investing
summary: An iShares presentation arguing sustainable (ESG) investing supplements traditional financial analysis. Volkswagen's 2015 emissions scandal, Vale's 2019 Brumadinho dam disaster (3B gallons, ~300 deaths, stock -20%), and Equifax's 2017 data breach (150M people) illustrate ESG risks traditional financials missed. Sustainable ETFs grew at 80% CAGR since 2015; 92% of S&P 500 firms publish sustainability reports vs 20% in 2011. iShares ESG ETFs: ESGU, EAGG, ICLN, SDG.

title: How to build a DIY portfolio with ETFs
summary: Walks DIY investors through ETF portfolio construction: starting early to harness compounding, the case against market timing, diversification across stocks and bonds, asset allocation (e.g. 60/40), and rebalancing. Surveys ETF categories — core (S&P 500-style index), asset allocation, factor (value/quality/momentum/size/min-vol), thematic/megatrend, and active — plus what to look at when evaluating any fund (manager, structure, exposure, cost).

title: Thematic investing with ETFs
summary: iShares presentation on thematic/megatrend investing. Single stocks (eToys vs Amazon) are too risky; broad indexes (NASDAQ Composite ~6x in 20 years) are too diffuse; theme-specific ETFs are the Goldilocks middle (MSCI e-commerce benchmark ~27x). Surveys five BlackRock megatrends: technological breakthroughs, demographics/genomics, rapid urbanization, climate transition, emerging global wealth. Lists iShares megatrend ETFs: IHAK, IRBO, IDNA, IFRA, ICLN, IDRV, CNYA, and XT for one-trade access.

title: The Psychology of Investing
summary: iShares presentation on behavioral finance: common biases that derail investors. Examples include Fairfield Sentry (Bernie Madoff's feeder fund) for envy/FOMO, S&P envy (diversified portfolios always lag), lottery effect, and loss aversion. Cites Isaac Newton's £20K South Sea Company loss (~$3M today) — intelligence doesn't protect against emotion. Argues a disciplined written plan or financial advisor is the main protection.

title: New Series, Episode 1: iShares Market Mythbusting with Gargi Chaudhuri
summary: Gargi Chaudhuri opens the iShares Market Mythbusting series by addressing common investor myths: that successful investing requires expertise, that comparisons to past market eras (1970s, 1980s) are reliable, and that timing the market matters more than time in it. Counters with the argument that ETFs democratize access for inexperienced investors and that long-term commitment beats trying to pick bottoms.

title: Market Mythbusting with Gargi Chaudhuri: Is it hard to start investing?
summary: Gargi Chaudhuri addresses whether starting to invest is hard: argues that picking individual winners consistently is nearly impossible, but ETFs do that research and exposure work for investors. Suggests rotating at the margin toward more attractive areas of the market while staying diversified. Notes that thematic investing (sub-sectors, themes, countries) is valid when not over-concentrated.

title: Jay Jacob's A Walk Through Thematic Investing Series: Artificial Intelligence
summary: Jay Jacobs walks through how investors can access the AI megatrend via thematic ETFs. Highlights AI's reach across industries (energy efficiency, medicine, agriculture, content delivery) and projects $13 trillion in additional economic output by 2030 from AI users. Surveys three iShares thematic ETFs: IRBO (global robotics and AI), SOXX (US semiconductors), IDRV (autonomous vehicles).

title: Bonds are Back: Gargi's Marketing Mythbusting
summary: Gargi Chaudhuri explains what bonds are — investments paying scheduled interest — and busts the myth that they're only for retirees. After Federal Reserve rate hikes pushed yields higher, bonds serve many investor needs: inflation protection (TIPS ETFs), short-horizon savings (short-term bond ETFs), or stock-portfolio diversification (broad US bond market ETFs). Mentions LQD as an example of ~1,000 investment-grade corporate bonds accessible in a single ticker.

title: Market Mythbusting: Women and Investing
summary: Gargi Chaudhuri argues fewer women invest due to structural factors (representation, the gender pay gap, longer lifespans, caregiving time) rather than lack of interest. Cites that women globally earn ~37% less than men in similar roles and that if women invested at the same rate as men, an extra $3 trillion in assets would enter markets. Notes statistics showing women tend to be more diligent investors with better risk-adjusted returns.

title: Five Mega Forces Reshaping the Future of Investing
summary: Survey of five "mega forces" iShares views as reshaping the global economy: demographic shifts (aging developed world, emerging-market booms), geopolitical tensions reshaping supply chains, the low-carbon energy transition, AI becoming the bedrock of corporate productivity, and the future of finance (how people transact, borrow, and seek returns). Positions iShares thematic ETFs as a way to capture these long-term forces.

title: iShares Market Mythbusting: Women and Retirement
summary: Two iShares strategists discuss structural challenges women face for retirement: the gender pay gap, women providing roughly 80% of US caregiving (with workforce interruptions), and longer lifespans requiring savings to stretch further. Highlights target-date ETFs as a flexible tool for retirement, home down payments, or college savings. Closes on balancing debt repayment against early investing, noting compounding rewards even small early contributions.

title: iShares Money Market ETFs
summary: Karen Veraperry, head of US fixed income strategy at iShares, announces the launch of iShares' first two money market ETFs: the iShares Government Money Market ETF (primarily short-term government securities) and the iShares Prime Money Market ETF (broader money-market instruments including corporate commercial paper and CDs). Both are actively managed within strict regulatory guidelines and packaged as ETFs for trading flexibility.

title: What is income investing?
summary: Joanna Abukaza defines income investing as strategies aiming to generate a steady portfolio income stream — accessible via bonds, dividend-paying stocks, or active strategies. Surveys four iShares income ETFs across these categories: HDV (high-dividend stocks), BALI (large-cap with call-option income overlay), USHY (high-yield corporate bonds), and BINC (active fixed-income, managed by BlackRock's global CIO Rick Rieder).

title: What should I look for before making an investment?
summary: Joanna Abukaza argues the most important pre-investment question is suitability — whether an investment fits your goals, time horizon, and risk tolerance. Professional investors apply this same framework: define what you're investing for, when you'll need the money, and your tolerance for volatility. Personal goals matter more than chasing fancy metrics or worrying about quarterly performance — a goals-first frame helps investors ignore the short-term Wall Street noise.

title: What are digital assets?
summary: Anna Nerys explains digital assets as anything in the digital world with monetary value — covering data, intellectual property, and cryptocurrencies. Distinguishes Bitcoin (the largest cryptocurrency by market value, using cryptography, peer-to-peer networks, and a public blockchain to verify transactions without intermediaries) from Ethereum (a technology platform enabling decentralized applications, with Ether as its native cryptocurrency).

title: What is the best ETF for playing defense?
summary: Caroline Bias addresses defensive investing in volatile markets, noting there's no single "best" defensive ETF since goals and risk tolerance differ. Surveys three iShares ETFs for different defensive needs: IAU (gold for macro/inflation hedge), BINC (active flexible-income for hard-to-reach yield), and BALI (large-cap with covered-call income for lower volatility than the broader market). Closes with the standard advice to stay invested rather than time rebounds.

title: Is it too late to invest in AI?
summary: Caroline Bias argues it's not too late to invest in AI — the theme is still in early stages despite explosive growth since ChatGPT's November 2022 debut. Frames AI's potential impact as comparable to the industrial revolution. Surveys two iShares ETFs for AI exposure: ARTY (Future AI and Tech, covering US and global AI-contributing companies) and SOXX (US-based semiconductor firms benefitting from the AI mega force).

title: How can iShares ETFs help investors meet their retirement goals?
summary: Anna Day outlines how iShares ETFs support the retirement journey from accumulation through decumulation. Stresses two universal truths: it's never too late to start (but earlier is better) and retirement plans must last through retirement, not just to it. Highlights three product families: LifePath target-date ETFs (auto-rebalancing toward the retirement year), outcome ETFs (navigating volatility), and iBonds ETFs (defined-maturity bonds for managing retirement spending).

title: How do you know which themes are most relevant?
summary: Ashley Sadler walks through BlackRock's five mega forces (AI, geopolitical fragmentation, demographics, the low-carbon transition, the future of finance) and addresses the harder follow-up: which themes to invest in and when. Positions the iShares US Thematic Rotation Active ETF (THRO) as a single fund that dynamically rotates among US market themes based on BlackRock's proprietary signals, sparing investors from trying to time individual theme rotations themselves.

title: How does international investing affect taxes here?
summary: John Angel addresses the tax question for US investors in international stocks: foreign taxes may apply but the IRS Foreign Tax Credit usually prevents double taxation. Cites three reasons for more international exposure: dollar weakness (~10% down YTD), US concentration risk (20 largest stocks drove 59% of the S&P 500's last-5-year return), and quality-factor diversification. Surveys IQLT, IXUS, and IDEV as iShares vehicles.

title: The Ask: How can I invest for dividend growth?
summary: John Angel argues dividend investing is timely now because the current decade — like the 1940s and 1970s — features rising rates, slowing growth, and geopolitical uncertainty. In those decades dividends contributed at least half of total stock-market return, vs ~15% or less in the 1990s and 2010s. Across the full period since 1926, 31% of the S&P 500's total return has come from dividends. Positions iShares DGRO as a way to access companies with a history of growing their dividends.

title: How can I get involved with crypto?
summary: Kevin Lee explains crypto investing via ETPs as a simpler alternative to direct purchases. Bitcoin (launched 2009) is by far the largest crypto by market cap; Ether is the second-largest and powers the Ethereum blockchain. Surveys iShares Bitcoin Trust (IBIT) and iShares Ethereum Trust (ETHA) as ways to gain exposure without direct-purchase burdens (storage, security, tax reporting). Cautions that crypto remains highly volatile and warrants small exposure.

title: The Ask: What are active ETFs?
summary: Kevin Lee defines active ETFs as a fast-growing category projected by BlackRock to triple global AUM to $4.2 trillion by 2030. Contrasts active ETFs with index ETFs (which seek to mirror an index): active-ETF managers use proprietary research and real-time decisions. Surveys three active-ETF categories: alpha-seeking, outcome (e.g., income maximization), and exposure (hard-to-index assets). Cites BALI (iShares US Large Cap Premium Income ETF) as an example active ETF.

title: The Four Seasons of Infrastructure
summary: A creative seasonal framing of infrastructure investing — broadband (fall back-to-school), natural gas (winter heating), toll roads (spring travel), and water utilities (summer). Cites specific projections including 88M US households adding broadband by 2029, electronic tolls growing 287% to $43B by 2035, and 20% summer revenue lifts for water utilities. Positions iShares infrastructure ETFs to capture these everyday cash flows.

title: The Ask: How will Fed rate cuts affect the performance of the Magnificent 7?
summary: Fay Witherell addresses how Fed rate cuts will affect the Magnificent 7. Notes the Fed cut 25bps on September 17 with more cuts expected. The Mag 7 (Alphabet, Amazon, Apple, Meta, Microsoft, Nvidia, Tesla) has outperformed the S&P 500 every year since 2014 except 2022. Q2 earnings grew 26.6% for Mag 7 vs 8.1% for the other 493 stocks. Argues large-cap growth historically outperforms post-cut. Positions BAI (iShares AI Innovation and Tech Active ETF) for selective AI exposure.

title: The Ask: How are Americans investing in 2025?
summary: Samantha Ricci shares findings from BlackRock's People and Money survey (5,000+ US adults). 24 million Americans already own ETFs and 19 million more are very likely to buy in the next 12 months — 44% would be first-time ETF investors. Of those first-timers, 50%+ plan equity ETFs and 47% plan crypto ETPs. First-time investors trend younger and under $100K income, suggesting ETFs are removing barriers to entry.

title: The Ask: Is AI in a bubble?
summary: Sam Mullen argues AI stocks are NOT in a bubble. Three reasons: (1) real demand for AI services exceeds compute supply, persisting for years; (2) AI capex is funded by cash flows from highly profitable companies, not borrowed money; (3) valuations are reasonable vs the dot-com peak — today's four largest AI hyperscalers (Microsoft, Alphabet, Amazon, Meta) trade at ~24x forward earnings vs 88x for Microsoft/Cisco/Intel/Oracle at the dot-com peak. Positions BAI and ARTY for exposure.

title: 2026 Market Outlook: Seven Key Questions
summary: Q&A with Gargi Chaudhuri on iShares' 2026 market outlook. Defines 2026 as: above-trend growth, easing policy, accelerating productivity, and a need for selectivity. AI remains dominant — $500B+ was spent on data centers in 2025, with trillions more expected through 2030. Concentration is the key risk: the largest AI-driven companies now exceed 40% of the S&P 500, so diversification matters more than ever. Argues for broader income sourcing and diversification beyond traditional stocks/bonds.

title: The Ask: Fed Outlook 2026: What it may mean for fixed income investors
summary: Karen Veraperry, head of iShares fixed income strategy, lays out a 2026 Fed and bond outlook. The Fed cut rates 25bps at each of its last three 2025 meetings; the team expects modest additional easing in 2026 dependent on inflation and labor data. Highlights three fixed-income opportunities: the belly of the yield curve via an iShares mid-duration Treasury ETF, BINC (active flexible income, managed by BlackRock CIO Rick Rieder), and SGOV (the market's largest Treasury ETF) for short-term cash.

title: The Ask: What’s the investment outlook for 2026?
summary: Annie Kana presents iShares' 2026 outlook: above-trend growth, easing policy, accelerating productivity, with risks from labor weakness, rich valuations, and Fed/geopolitical uncertainty. Favors selective risk-taking. AI remains top equity theme but non-AI opportunities are broadening. Mentions DYNF (dynamic factor rotation) plus diversifiers: bonds, EM stocks (especially Asia), gold, Bitcoin. Income from EM debt, securitized assets, dividends, options.

title: What Is an ETF? (Think of it Like an Appetizer Sampler)
summary: Anna walks through what an ETF is using a relatable restaurant-appetizer analogy. Global ETF assets exceed $11 trillion — far more than Taylor Swift's Eras Tour ticket sales and US pet spending combined. ETFs are exchange-traded bundles of investments (stocks, bonds, commodities) that offer diversification, intraday trading flexibility, and low cost. iShares has 450+ ETFs across broad indexes, international, thematic (AI, blockchain), and growth/dividend tilts.

title: What is Diversification? (Think of it like a playlist)
summary: Anna Nerys defines diversification as owning a variety of investments so you're not over-exposed to any one. Uses a playlist analogy: high-growth stocks are the bangers (Rihanna, Lady Gaga), high-quality bonds are the steady crowd-pleasers (Journey, Bon Jovi), and crypto/commodities are the wild-card track. Emphasizes low correlation across holdings — assets that don't all move together — and positions iShares' 450+ ETFs as a way to access diversified exposure with a handful of picks.

title: The Ask: Thematic Outlook
summary: Ashley Da frames the 2026 thematic outlook around convergence: AI, crypto, geopolitical fragmentation, and infrastructure intersecting. Two example themes: (1) computing conflict — AI shifts defense spending from physical to digital systems (accessible via ITA, the iShares US Aerospace and Defense ETF); (2) pipes and power — AI data centers and infrastructure need massive power (accessible via PWR, the iShares US Power Infrastructure ETF).

title: What is Long-Term Investing? (Think of it like a workout plan)
summary: Anna Nerys defines long-term investing as active and consistent — regular contributions sustained through market ups and downs. Uses a gym/workout analogy. Shows the difference between a savings account (avg ~0.4%) vs S&P 500 (avg ~11% over last two decades) over 30 years with $100/month: huge gap thanks to compounding. Argues diversification (like working every muscle group) is the foundation of any long-term plan and iShares ETFs make it simple.

title: What are dividends and why do investors use them?
summary: Explains what dividends are — cash payments companies make to shareholders out of profits — and how dividend yield is determined relative to stock price. Covers the two options for received dividends: taking them as cash income or reinvesting them to compound returns over time. Notes dividends aren't guaranteed and positions iShares Dividend ETFs as a diversified way to access dividend-paying companies.

title: What is bitcoin? And what is a bitcoin ETP? (Think of it like a bridge)
summary: Anna Nerys explains Bitcoin: a digital cryptocurrency running on blockchain, capped at 21 million coins ever, that enables peer-to-peer value transfer without banks. Bitcoin ETPs bridge traditional investing and crypto by holding Bitcoin in a custodied wrapper, sidestepping the need for users to manage private keys themselves. Two pizzas bought for ~$41 in 2010 would now be worth over $1 billion. Positions iShares Bitcoin Trust ETF as an accessible vehicle.

title: The Ask: What if you could reshape volatility vs. trying to avoid it?
summary: Kyle Axelrod argues derivative ("outcome") ETFs are especially relevant in volatile markets. They use options to target growth, income, or downside protection. Buffer ETFs like MMAX use options for up to 100% downside protection with market participation up to a cap over a one-year period — giving up some upside for downside cushion. Helps investors stay invested through volatility, since the market's best days cluster during volatile periods.

Why You Still Can’t Retire (Even If You Have Enough)
Stop Overfunding Your 401(k). Do This Instead
You Don’t Need a Financial Advisor… Until You Do (Here’s When)
You’re Thinking About Social Security Wrong… It’s Not About Not 62 vs. 67 vs. 70
$15M in Nvidia Stock Case Study | Don't Just "Diversify Everything"
Retire at 60? Watch This Before You Claim Social Security
Taxes on a $3M Retirement Portfolio (What You'll Actually Owe Each Year)
Why I Told My Client Not to Pay Off Their Mortgage Before Retiring
The Real Math of Working 1 More Year (It’s Not What You Think)
If You Only Watch One Retirement Video, Make it This
7 Investments That Will REDUCE Your Taxes Immediately
summary: Dave Zoller walks through seven investments that reduce taxes. Four common ones (oil and gas partnerships, real estate, municipal bonds, traditional IRA/401k) and three less-discussed (HSA, small business, donor-advised fund / impact investing). For each, he scores tax deduction on contribution, tax on income, and tax on sale. Recommends impact investing via a DAF as the best-deduction option since the full contribution is deductible immediately.

title: I Retired With Millions BUT Made 6 BIG Mistakes
summary: Dave Zoller walks through six post-sale mistakes business owner client 'Dan' made transitioning into retirement: (1) underestimating loss of control, (2) lifestyle-expense changes when business deductions vanish, (3) jumping into 'attractive' deals too fast, (4) missing pre-sale tax strategies like charitable pre-funding, (5) skipping asset-protection planning, (6) handing wealth to unprepared heirs. Opens with Arthur Brooks' fluid-vs-crystallized intelligence frame.

title: They Retired 10 Years Early Due To 3 Things
summary: Dave Zoller walks through a real-client case: Mary and John, 58 with $2M saved, told by a CFP they had to work 12 more years before retiring. Reframes the planning conversation around life values FIRST (faith, family, fitness, fun, friends) rather than numbers-first. After exploring what-if scenarios (move to South Carolina to be near grandkids, drop $25K/yr country club, reduce travel spend), the plan shifted from 12 more years to ~2 — same risk tolerance, totally different outcome.

title: Leadership, Transitions and Legacy Impact w/ Bill Holbrook
summary: Long-form Wealth Beyond Riches podcast interview with Bill Holbrook, executive coach to Streamline Financial. Covers Bill's path from youth pastor to executive coach, how he uses StrengthsFinder/Myers-Briggs/Enneagram as dynamic assessments rather than boxes, identity loss in retirement (the 'RW word'), the importance of leaving a leadership legacy via succession planning (2-4 year timeline), defining 'enough' to resist scaling, and modeling rhythms of rest (daily/weekly/sabbatical vacations).

title: 4 Uncomfortable Retirement Truths You Need To Hear
summary: Dave Zoller shares four uncomfortable retirement truths that distinguish fulfilled retirees from those who struggle: (1) financial success doesn't guarantee retirement success — identity, purpose, and health matter as much; (2) family caregiving for elderly parents is a common, often overlooked retirement-time cost; (3) retirement isn't a stress-free vacation — passive-income stress and news anxiety emerge; (4) the saver-to-spender transition is harder than expected even with abundant savings.

title: 4 Reasons To Do ROTH Conversions If You Have A Pension
summary: Dave Zoller argues retirees with pensions have a stronger case for Roth conversions than those without. Three reasons: (1) pensions plus Social Security plus RMDs at 73 can push retirees into higher brackets they don't need the cash for; (2) higher taxable income drives Medicare IRMAA surcharges; (3) the surviving spouse's tax filing status (single vs married) compounds the bracket impact. Recommends using the pre-RMD/pre-Social-Security window as the Roth conversion 'sweet spot.'

title: 9 Things to STOP DOING After 55 To Enjoy Life More
summary: Dave Zoller lists nine things the happiest retirees they've worked with STOPPED doing after 55 to increase retirement satisfaction. The list: stop taking time for granted (life-in-weeks visualization), stop saving unnecessarily, stop compromising health, stop worrying so much, stop spending time with draining people, stop spending on things not aligned with values, stop over-supporting adult children, stop pretending to be someone you're not, stop living with regret.

title: He Never Expected THIS Problem In Retirement
summary: Dave Zoller shares a real client (early-50s retiree, one year in) who faced unexpected social pressure: family and acquaintances kept saying he was too young, asking what he'd do all day. Reframes the answer with three practices — rest, test, learn — plus play. Highlights investing not just money but also TIME in three areas often missed by financial advice: curiosities (hobbies/passions), relationships, and contribution to others.

title: The Journey of Reinvention: Mikey Taylor's Path from Pro Skateboarding to Real Estate
summary: Wealth Beyond Riches interview with Mikey Taylor — former pro skateboarder, Saint Archer co-founder (sold to MillerCoors), now real-estate fund manager at Commune Capital. Covers his identity collapse after losing his skate sponsor, year-long Bible reading reset, what financial freedom means, building parallel brand equity, real-estate macro (multifamily, storage, hospitality), and his next chapter on marriage retreats.

title: Vanguard Predicts It Won't Be As Good Moving Forward
summary: Dave Zoller breaks down Vanguard's decade outlook (~2024-2034): US bonds projected to return 4.8-5.8% nominal annualized (much higher than pre-rate-hikes); international bonds 4.7-5.7%. 60/40 portfolio's probability of hitting 7%+ annualized increased. Vanguard sees US equities (especially large-cap growth) overvalued, with emerging markets and small-cap potentially undervalued. Cautions that Vanguard's 2014 decade forecast called for 5-8% global equity returns — actual was ~12%/yr.

title: Common Withdrawal Mistakes Retirees Make
summary: Dave Zoller covers three common retirement-withdrawal mistakes: (1) following the default 'taxable first, then tax-deferred, then Roth' order without modeling — can lead to forced early Social Security and inflated RMDs; (2) not mapping fluctuating spending across go-go vs slow-go years; (3) misaligning investments with the income plan, so a sequence-of-returns shock forces selling at a loss. Cites the Vanguard Advisor Alpha study claiming withdrawal-order choice is worth up to 1.2%/yr.

title: When To Start Collecting Social Security Benefits
summary: Dave Zoller walks through a visual decision-tree for when to start Social Security (62, full retirement age, or 70), using a hypothetical 63-year-old retired example. Shows the key flaw — the diagram ignores spousal/survivor-benefit dynamics. Then introduces a JPMorgan longevity-probability chart and a return-vs-longevity grid: every year deferred past full retirement age boosts benefits ~8%, so compare against your expected portfolio return.

title: Don't Let This Happen In Retirement
summary: Dave Zoller argues that the 'stress' retirees lose at work isn't all bad — beneficial eustress (challenge, progress toward goals) disappears too, and its absence is linked to faster mental/physical decline. Issues a 30-for-30 challenge: pick an area of growth, commit 30 minutes a day for 30 consecutive days, track on a paper calendar, and tell someone for accountability.

title: 2025 Social Security Increase
summary: Dave Zoller delivers the 2025 Social Security COLA estimate (~2.6%, down from 3.2% in 2024 and 8.7% in 2023) and walks through three core retirement-income risks: longevity (running out of money), inflation, and exposure to bad markets. Notes Social Security helps with all three; pensions and certain annuities address one or two. Recap of the 1982 Social Security fix as evidence Congress will likely paper over the 2033 trust-fund shortfall.

title: Unlocking Integrity and Generosity: A Journey with John Blumberg
summary: Wealth Beyond Riches interview with John Blumberg — former 18-year Arthur Andersen partner turned leadership coach and author of Return on Integrity. Covers leaving Andersen five years before its Enron collapse, redefining integrity as wholeness, the difference between estate planning and wealth transfer, why defining 'when is enough enough' prevents wealth from gripping you, and the importance of 'drift catchers' who tell you the truth.

title: The Month You Retire Really Matters
summary: Dave Zoller breaks down how the month you retire can affect your finances and well-being. Covers three categories: tax benefits (retiring in the first half of the year opens Roth-conversion, tax-gain-harvesting, and 401(k)-max-out options), non-financial benefits (spring/summer retirement avoids Northern-state winter depression), and financial considerations (pension full-eligibility dates, Medicare/COBRA timing, year-end bonuses, accrued PTO).

title: RETIREMENT EXPERT: They'll Be Caught Off Guard When This Happens In Retirement - Ric Edelman
summary: Dave Zoller interviews Ric Edelman (founder of Edelman Financial Engines, ~$250B AUM) on the future of retirement. Edelman argues longevity (likely 95-100 for those alive in 2030) makes the traditional 60s retirement and 60/40 portfolio obsolete. Recommends 70/30 or 75/25 well into the 80s, ~20% allocation to exponential technologies (AI, robotics, biotech, blockchain/crypto, including 1-3% in Bitcoin), and reinventing for the next 30-40-year chapter via new.

title: Why Most Retirees Secretly Struggle
summary: Dave Zoller covers the four most common reasons retirees secretly struggle: (1) time-alone discomfort (cites the University of Virginia electric-shock-button study where 67% of men shocked themselves rather than sit alone); (2) the wealth-health-time triangle imbalance — the equilateral-triangle phase in the 50s-60s is the magical retirement window; (3) the spending paradox (Kitces study: 2/3 of 4% retirees end with more than 2x their starting principal); (4).

title: The Intersection of Wellness and Leadership with Jackie Glass
summary: Tim hosts executive wellness coach Jackie Glass on Wealth Beyond Riches. They explore why leaders are loneliest during transitions (not at the top), introduce Carey Lohrenz's prepare/perform/prevail framework for life change, and discuss the practice of 'margin' and the principle of working from a place of rest rather than resting from a place of work. Closes with stories from the Global Leadership Summit, including hosting Joni Eareckson Tada.

title: One BAD Decision Ruined His Retirement
summary: Dave Zoller tells the story of two retirees who started March 2020 with similar plans: Jane and 'Kevin.' Both panicked during the 30%+ drop, but Jane focused on the seven-year safe-money bucket and held; Kevin sold to cash and never got back in. Jane then layered three winning moves — tax-loss harvesting, a Roth conversion at depressed values, and a system-driven rebalance from bonds into stocks. Kevin missed the recovery and his plan suffered.

title: How Recognizing Your Strengths Can Transform Your Life with Jackie Glass
summary: Tim continues with Jackie Glass on Wealth Beyond Riches, focused on Patrick Lencioni's Working Genius assessment (Wonder, Invention, Discernment, Galvanizing, Enablement, Tenacity). Jackie reveals Tim's two geniuses are Enablement and Tenacity, his competencies are Wonder and Discernment, and his frustrations are Galvanizing and Invention.

title: Spousal Social Security Explained (w/ Examples)
summary: Dave Zoller explains spousal Social Security: the spousal benefit is up to 50% of the higher earner's full retirement age benefit but is reduced if claimed before the spouse's own FRA. Key rules: the spouse claiming a spousal benefit can't claim until the higher earner has; the spousal benefit is based on the claimant's own age, not the spouse's; divorced spouses (married 10+ years, not remarried) can claim on an ex's record without the ex knowing or being affected.

title: Ideal Order of Successful Retirement Plans
summary: Dave Zoller lays out the order Streamline uses for successful retirement plans: (1) start with the income plan (vision of retirement, then numbers), not investments or risk tolerance; (2) let the income plan drive the investment plan; (3) control what's controllable — taxes (tax-efficient funds and tax-loss harvesting), costs (lower-fee fiduciary advice), and risk (preparing for all four economic seasons rather than guessing).

title: Discovering True Wealth: Dave Zoller's Journey Beyond Finances
summary: Tim interviews his business partner Dave Zoller on Wealth Beyond Riches about Dave's career trajectory — from tax-prep cubicle at his dad's firm to wealth advisor to YouTube content lead. Discusses Dave's transition into the teaching seat, the principle of finding what you're good at and gifted at (vs. trying to do everything), Arthur Brooks' fluid vs. crystallized intelligence, and the servant-first / human-first approach that distinguishes Streamline from sales-driven advisory firms.

title: If you're in your 50s or 60s, watch this. Life Lessons from 70-year-olds
summary: Dave Zoller compiles 10 pieces of life advice from 70-year-old clients to people in their 50s: don't miss the magical equal-sided wealth/health/time triangle window; prioritize three types of time (career, relationships, recovery); learn to say no; time is more valuable than money; invest in fitness and mental health (one 69-year-old client looked a decade younger after restarting yoga and meditation); avoid the status-symbol trap (Porsche client wished he had.

title: Redefining Success: Dave Zoller's Approach to Money and Life
summary: Tim continues with Dave Zoller on Wealth Beyond Riches. Dave discusses his philosophy that money is important but should never be the main thing — treat it as a steward and helper rather than master. Covers servant mindset learned from his dad Steve, three Cs as the framework for retirement fulfillment, finding strengths via StrengthsFinder, AI's coming impact on financial advice (par-planner roles at risk; relational human-first advisors will thrive), and the.

title: Successful Retirees Track FOUR Numbers
summary: Dave Zoller identifies the four numbers successful retirees track: (1) fixed costs / essentials — needs vs. wants for the income plan; (2) net worth — quarterly snapshot of overall financial health; (3) investment allocation — stocks/bonds/cash mix by account; (4) years of safe-money balance — conservative assets converted into years of withdrawals (e.g., $180K / $60K = 3 years). Recommends Monarch Money, YNAB, or Tiller for expense tracking.

title: IRS Releases New 2025 Tax Brackets + Tax Sunsets
summary: Dave Zoller summarizes the IRS's 2025 inflation adjustments — a smaller-than-usual 2.8% bracket bump, $30,000 standard deduction for married filing jointly, and a $19,000 gift tax exclusion. Then walks through seven Tax Cuts and Jobs Act provisions scheduled to sunset at the end of 2025: rising rates (negative), smaller standard deduction (negative), returning personal exemption (positive), lower child tax credit (negative), uncapped SALT deduction (positive for.

title: Ask Your Spouse Before Retirement
summary: Dave Zoller walks a couple through a guided exercise of conversation prompts to discuss with their spouse before retirement — designed to take ~10 minutes of pause-and-write between questions. Covers the three Cs (creativity/curiosity, connection, contribution), what they want to do that they can't now, what they'd miss about work, confidence ratings, location preferences, role with adult children/grandkids, deepest connections to build, and contribution.

title: Saying 'Yes' to Change: Kristen Welch's Journey from Blogger to Global Nonprofit Leader
summary: Tim interviews Kristen Welch, CEO and founder of Mercy House Global, a Christian nonprofit that runs three maternity homes in Nairobi, Kenya and a fair-trade artisan supply chain providing dignified work to women escaping survival prostitution and human trafficking. They cover Kristen's path from frustrated writer to mom-blogger to nonprofit founder, the practice of gratitude as a discipline, why she lives by a 'rule of life' that includes daily silence, solitude,.

title: New 401K Rules Are Coming
summary: Dave Zoller walks through three big 401(k) changes coming in 2025 from the SECURE Act 2.0: (1) ages 60-63 can contribute an extra $11,250 catch-up bringing the cap to $34,750; (2) a US Department of Labor searchable database for forgotten stranded 401(k)s (~$1.3 trillion estimated); (3) employer match can be directed to student loans, an HSA, or an HRA instead of the 401(k).

title: How Trump Can Impact Your Financial Future
summary: Dave Zoller analyzes how the 2024 Trump election outcome (Republican president plus Republican House and Senate) could affect markets, taxes, inflation, and Social Security. Data shows party in power has no statistically significant effect on S&P 500 returns (1933-2022). With Republican control, the 2017 TCJA tax cuts are now more likely to extend past 2025, giving more flexibility for Roth conversions, capital-gains harvesting, and charitable bunching with.

title: Burnout, Sabbatical, and Renewed Leadership with Kristen Welch
summary: Tim continues the conversation with Kristen Welch on Wealth Beyond Riches about her three-month sabbatical taken right after becoming CEO of Mercy House Global. After 13 years of nonprofit founding and a series of crises (warehouse collapse, fire at a Kenya home), she stepped away — spent the first 10 minutes in silence having a panic attack, broke her foot on day one, then unwound layers of anger and reactivity.

title: 7 Things To Do Before 2024 Ends
summary: Dave Zoller covers seven year-end retirement actions for 2024: (1) review Medicare/ACA open enrollment (Oct 15 to Dec 7); (2) tax-gain harvesting (0% LTCG bracket for MFJ ≤ ~$94K) and tax-loss harvesting (offsets gains; mind wash-sale rule); (3) check mutual-fund year-end capital-gain distributions before they hit your 1099; (4) charitable giving via Donor-Advised Funds for current-year deduction; (5) Qualified Charitable Distributions for those 70+ to satisfy RMDs.

title: Psychology Expert: 5 Simple Steps To Retire Happy
summary: Dave Zoller interviews Dr. Daniel Crosby (chief behavioral officer, author of 'The Soul of Wealth') on five psychological keys to retiring happy. Covers Martin Seligman's PERMA model (Positive emotion, Engagement, Relationships, Meaning, Achievement) and how retirement removes E/R/M/A — even when P is funded; the 'premeditation of evils' (Stoic premortem) for risk-proofing a plan; pairing spending with relationships (the car-club example) for enduring happiness;.

title: You Don’t Need Permission: Pam Marmon on Entrepreneurship, Financial Wellness, and World Schooling
summary: Tim interviews Pam Marmon — best-selling author, organizational change consultant, mother of three young boys, and worldschooling parent — on Wealth Beyond Riches. They discuss building family vision and values like an organization, why she 'no longer does' certain things (no full-time projects, no hourly billing, no on-site client work), her path from communist-Bulgaria childhood to Fortune 500 to solopreneur, the freedom of an explicit 'enough number,' and using.

title: This Makes Retirement Harder
summary: Dave Zoller explains why modern retirement often feels boring after the honeymoon phase. The five stages of retirement include a 'disenchantment' phase that produces a mini-depression after the initial excitement fades. Neuroscience: pleasure and pain share brain circuitry, and chronic easy pleasures (TV, scrolling) drive a 'dopamine deficit state.' Counter it with three practices: set small daily goals (steps, language module), take on a meaningful project.

title: DON'T Retire Yet - There's Another Way
summary: Dave Zoller argues phased retirement is an under-used alternative to a full stop. Walks through financial benefits (delay withdrawals, keep contributing, 6-8%/yr Social Security boost from delayed filing) and non-financial benefits (purpose, structure, social contact). Three client examples: a 65-year-old extrovert who took a flexible Trader Joe's job, a man who started a small side business with a friend, and a 60s business owner who restructured to work 15 hours a week.

title: Did Warren Buffett Just Issue A Warning For 2025?
summary: Dave Zoller addresses headlines that Buffett/Berkshire is sitting on a record ~$280B in cash and whether retirees should worry. Argues the comparison doesn't apply: Berkshire is an insurance conglomerate needing $30-80B in reserves, has different scale constraints, and a perpetual horizon. Recommends a three-step retiree process: income plan first, then investment plan, then a 2-3 year cash bucket — and cites Buffett's own 90/10 S&P 500 advice for his family.

title: Stop Letting Phones Steal Your Days: Joey Odom on Digital Wellness, Time Freedom, and Leadership
summary: Tim Meisenheimer interviews Joey Odom (co-founder of Aro) on Wealth Beyond Riches about digital wellness. Odom shares his pivot from commercial real estate to Aro, the 5 S's framework (start with self, two hours phone-free a day, sacred times/places, search for moments of connection, stop at nothing), and Aro's shift to a B2B church/wellness-benefit model. Aro users average ~1 month/year reclaimed off their phones; the company has logged over 2 million phone-free hours.

title: 7 Financial Goals Everyone Should Hit By 60
summary: Dave Zoller lists seven financial goals to hit by age 60, in order: (1) move from emergency fund to a retirement cash management system (checking-account floor of ~3 months); (2) track expenses and split needs vs wants; (3) track net worth; (4) be debt-free; (5) map future cash flow; (6) draft a real retirement plan; (7) button up the estate plan. Recommends Boldin (formerly NewRetirement) as the best DIY consumer planning tool.

title: The One Cost You Must Cut In Retirement
summary: Dave Zoller covers small-but-recurring retirement money leaks: (1) call your bank — many retirees earn ~0% on cash that could earn $5-10K/yr more in a high-yield option; (2) audit monthly subscriptions using Monarch Money or Rocket Money; (3) use AARP discounts (30% car rentals, 20% hotels) even if you 'feel young'; (4) the 'must cut' item — the Amazon one-click habit. Suggests a 24-hour rule before purchases.

title: This Is The BEST Year To Retire - Here's Why
summary: Dave Zoller argues 2025 is the best year to retire and reframes retirement as a 'Renaissance' — the convergence of resources, freedom, and time. Tackles three barriers: (1) the spending paradox (~67% of retirees double their principal under 4%); (2) health span vs lifespan (per Peter Attia: lifespan ~78, health span ~64); (3) global uncertainty (the timeline of 'reasons not to retire' is endless — uncertainty is a feature, not a bug). Recommends Boldin for DIY planning.

title: Navigating Business Exits and Leadership with Travis Hall
summary: Tim Meisenheimer interviews Travis Hall (serial entrepreneur, 11 startups, exited Remodel Health) on the day his sale closes. Covers the emotional reality of exit (identity, grief, money mattering less than expected), why founders need a next thing before selling, transparency with employees, sharing proceeds with non-cap-table staff, Friday Fails as culture practice, and pre-funding a donor-advised fund before the transaction closes.

title: Can Double-Digit Returns Happen Again in 2025?
summary: Dave Zoller addresses a subscriber question on whether 2025 could deliver a third straight year of double-digit S&P 500 returns (2023: ~26%, 2024: ~23%). Historical data: seven instances of three-or-more consecutive double-digit-return years since 1950, averaging every ~9.5 years with a 30-year gap from 1965-1995. Recommends focusing on what you can control: all-weather portfolio, conservative-bucket allocation, regular rebalancing.

title: Give Me 10 Minutes, I'll Transform Your Retirement Plan
summary: Dave Zoller introduces a neuroscience-based 'Retirement Mastery' worksheet for the non-financial side of retirement planning. Top half contrasts the ideal retirement (specific morning, hobbies, contributions, perfect Tuesday) against the 'anti-retirement' (worst-case version) to engage both carrot and stick in the limbic system. Bottom half identifies daily/weekly habits to start and to stop. Closes with the Streamline C3 model: creativity/curiosity, connection, contribution.

title: This Secret Money Habit Changes Everything
summary: Dave Zoller shares the one trait of the financially happiest retirees he's worked with: gratitude. Specifically, the practice of 'thank you money' (Ken Honda's Arigato money) — taking 3 seconds to mentally thank money both when it comes in (Social Security, distributions) AND when it goes out (paying bills, treating a friend to coffee). Argues many retirees thank God; others the universe; the practice shifts money from a stressor to a friend.

title: Retirement BEFORE 60 - What They Don't Talk About (Real Example)
summary: Dave Zoller walks through client John, mid-50s tech designer with two teens, burned out after a 25-year startup-to-corp arc. Wanted to retire but it didn't feel right. After a 30-minute call, reframed retirement as a 'sabbatical' (his wife's word) focused on what's 'fascinating and motivating' (AI). Recommended modeling 40 years as if no future paycheck — freedom to pick work without economics as driver. Introduces the 'mini retirements' concept.

title: Beyond the Hustle: Finding Fulfillment in Leadership & Business with Ben Hardman & Luke Dooley
summary: Tim Meisenheimer interviews Ben Hardman and Luke Dooley, co-founders of Banded — a peer-group community for business owners focused on the inner life (their thesis: leadership is 75% who you are, 25% what you do). They discuss the 'invisible click track' of ownership, founder isolation, story-based onboarding for trust, Montana retreats, the entrepreneur journey through 'depths of despair,' and David Brooks's 'the wealthier I became, the more I purchased loneliness.'

title: This Makes Retirement SO Much Better
summary: Dave Zoller introduces the 'retirement dress rehearsal' (credited to Cody Garrett of Measure Twice Financial): 6-12 months before retiring, direct your paycheck to brokerage and set a monthly transfer to checking equal to your planned retirement income. Tests the budget, eases the saver-to-spender switch, and provides error-correction time while employment safety-nets the simulation. Built around John and Mary, who 3 months in were only using half of what they could afford.

title: My 3 Step Retirement Formula
summary: Dave Zoller's 3-step retirement formula: (1) write down the monthly dollar amount you want, multiply by 12 — guess if needed; (2) subtract guaranteed income (usually Social Security); (3) divide the gap by 4% to get the investment target. Worked example: $6K/month × 12 = $72K need; -$24K SS = $48K gap; ÷ 4% = $1.2M target. Caveats: the 4% rule has flaws, tax-efficient income planning and inflation hedging matter.

title: Money, Meaning & Making a Difference with Jessica Jackley
summary: Tim Meisenheimer interviews Jessica Jackley (co-founder of Kiva, USC social entrepreneurship professor) on generosity. Covers the Altadena fires displacing her family, broken giving systems (uneven GoFundMe outcomes, private firefighting), the ~$300B/yr volunteer economy as wildly under-designed, her Kiva origin (accidental entrepreneur, microloans), and her own money-scarcity beliefs (her mother's tutoring hours commoditizing her time).

title: This Hidden Retirement Trap Ruins Your Best Years
summary: Dave Zoller addresses the 'retirement trap' — studies show 20-40% of retirees experience depression after leaving work. Introduces a 'retirement purpose portfolio' diversified across health, relationships, growth, spiritual, contribution, and leisure — parallel to a diversified financial portfolio. Notes the hedonic-treadmill problem and that meaningful part-time work without financial stress is a valid path.

title: Trump's Tariff Impact On Retirement - Client Video
summary: Dave Zoller's April 7, 2025 client video on tariff-driven market volatility (S&P down 10-15%). Reassures clients their portfolios have a conservative bucket holding 5-10 years of income (flat to positive YTD). Notes that during recording the S&P dropped 4-5%, jumped +8% in minutes on a fake tariff-pause leak, then dropped back — showing timing is impossible. Streamline has been through dot-bust, 2008, and the pandemic.

title: The Future of Meaningful Work in the Age of AI with Dan Cumberland
summary: Tim Meisenheimer interviews Dan Cumberland (founder of The Meaning Movement, AI consultant) on purpose, AI, and money. Dan untangles a 'youth pastor calling' that was actually his youth pastor's voice; offers the 4-P framework for next-chapter work (Person, Process, Product, Payoff); shares using AI to choose Mérida, Mexico for a school year; and is transparent about money — most generous at $30K/yr, harder now, trying to model abundance for his kids.

title: Most Retirees Don’t Realize This - Fritz Gilbert
summary: Dave Zoller interviews Fritz Gilbert (The Retirement Manifesto, author of Keys to a Successful Retirement) seven years into retirement. Covers the shock of unstructured days, the 9/10 rule (planning is 90% financial; retirement flips to 90% non-financial), the bucket strategy (3 years cash, 6 years bonds in a ladder, 70% growth), the 'force-yourself-to-spend' year-end rule, and 28% retiree depression vs 72% happiness anchored on 3.6 close friends.

title: How to Ruin Your 60s in 220 Seconds
summary: Dave Zoller delivers a satirical 'how to ruin your 60s in 220 seconds' list of 8 sarcastic-but-pointed anti-habits from people in their 70s and 80s: treat your body like a rental car, hoard like Scrooge, refuse to learn, become a full-time worrier, ignore spiritual life, master procrastination, isolate like Howard Hughes, and make your post-career identity crisis everyone else's problem. Closes with the truth: 60s are when time, health, and resources rarely align — don't waste them.

title: The 3 Types of Retirees: Which One Are You?
summary: Dave Zoller presents three retirement-purpose personalities: the Discoverer (purpose already exists, needs uncovering — start with prayer, life story, talk to old friends), the Creator (purpose is a blank canvas — write a vision statement, list 5 new experiences, do 30-day experiments), and the Pragmatist (no grand purpose needed — focus on sustainable daily routines and ordinary joy). Argues the mistake isn't choosing the wrong path — it's never choosing one and getting paralyzed.

title: Winning at What Truly Matters with John Olinger
summary: Tim Meisenheimer interviews John Olinger (10 years at Nike, author of Worthy Wins) on aligning time, energy, and attention. Olinger shares his dad's death at 74 (realizing at 37 he might be half-done), leading Nike's marketing for Kobe Bryant's legacy after the 2020 crash, and his framework: a meaningful story (personal, intentional, challenging, purposeful) plus a strategic plan. Example: 'wine Wednesdays' + Friday planning grew weekly couple touch-points from 1 to 3.

title: Your 401(k) Isn't Enough for Retirement
summary: Dave Zoller outlines three hidden 401(k) flaws (tax trap, inflation, RMDs) and a multi-bucket retirement strategy using tax-deferred, Roth, brokerage, and HSA accounts. Cites a Vanguard study claiming a tax-efficient withdrawal sequence can add up to 1% of annual return. Steps: max HSA, get 401(k) match, fund Roth IRA, then taxable brokerage, with Roth conversions during the post-retirement / pre-Social-Security sweet spot.

title: This Can Make or Break Your Retirement
summary: Dave Zoller introduces Martin Seligman's PERMA model (Positive emotion, Engagement, Relationships, Meaning, Achievement) as a framework for retirement fulfillment beyond money. References his prior interview with psychologist Daniel Crosby. Argues most retirement planning focuses only on the P (positive experiences / fun money), which is why some millionaires feel miserable in retirement while modestly-saving retirees thrive — all five elements must work together.

title: Don't Retire Until You Hit These 5 Goals
summary: Dave Zoller lists five goals to hit before retiring beyond just the financial number: (1) strengthen relationships now because work friendships vanish — studies show retirees lose 25% of social connections in the first 2 years; (2) build a life plan for the 168 hours/week of new free time; (3) clarify your identity beyond work; (4) honestly assess and address health impact of your job; (5) have a detailed retirement financial plan covering taxes, income, and estate planning.

title: Don't Waste Your Retirement (What The Happiest Retirees Do) The HC3 Method
summary: Dave Zoller argues the bigger retirement threat than running out of money is running out of meaning. Cites Bronnie Ware's #1 deathbed regret. Presents the HC3 method (Health, Create, Connect, Contribute) — four daily practices of the happiest retirees: contribute to others, create something daily for that progress feeling, connect with God or others, and prioritize daily movement. Personal story of asking his neurologist about his career to spark a meaningful interaction.

title: How The New Tax Law Impacts Your Retirement - Big Beautiful Bill
summary: Dave Zoller breaks down the new 2025 tax bill in plain English. Lower brackets are now permanent (the 2017 cuts were set to expire). Standard deduction rises to $31,500 married / $15,750 single. New senior bonus deduction ($6K per person 65+, phased out at higher AGI) runs 2025-2028 only. SALT cap raised from $10K to $40K. Charitable deduction available even for non-itemizers starting 2026 ($2K married / $1K single). Estate tax exemption raised to $15M per person.

title: The Retirement Assets Most People Ignore
summary: Dave Zoller shares the four-assets exercise learned from Ron Bulis at LifeWorks Advisors. Beyond financial assets, there are three others: human (faith, health, relationships, character, time), intellectual (skills, wisdom, experience), and community (church, neighbors, volunteer work, legacy). Less than 10% of clients pick financial as one of their top two when asked which two they'd keep — yet advisors spend 90% of their time on the financial bucket.

title: This Tax "Bunching" Tip Could Save You Thousands
summary: Dave Zoller explains how the new SALT cap increase (from $10K to $40K under the 2025 tax bill) combined with a 'bunching' strategy can save thousands. Bunching = alternating years between itemizing and standard deduction. Examples: prepay next year's property tax to itemize in the current year, or pre-fund 2-3 years of charitable giving via a donor-advised fund to take a single large deduction in one year while still satisfying the charity over time.

title: The Business Secret For Every Successful Retirement
summary: Dave Zoller argues the most successful retirees treat retirement like a business, not a vacation: clear systems for income (revenue), cash reserves (emergency fund), cash-flow tracking (P&L), team/accountability (board), and reinvesting in what matters (mission). Story of an anxious first-year retiree who regained confidence not from more money but from imposing structure. Recommends 1-3 years of cash reserves and monthly auto-transfers as a 'retirement paycheck.'

title: Why You Feel So Stuck In Retirement
summary: Dave Zoller describes the psychological transition of retirement, drawing on Ernest Becker's The Denial of Death. Two phases: the Builder's Last Stand (60-70, full energy, drive to accomplish — trap is trying to keep building the same way) and the Legacy Maker (passing on what you've already built). Three-step transition: outgo (drop draining relationships/commitments), redefine value (from production to wisdom-sharing), choose your legacy (mentoring, volunteering,.

title: Build A Retirement Paycheck That Works in ANY Market
summary: Dave Zoller walks through the three-bucket retirement income strategy used during March 2020 with a client who'd just retired and panicked during the COVID crash. Bucket 1 = 1-3 years of expenses in cash/cash-equivalents; Bucket 2 = 5-8 years in income-producing/conservative investments (dividends and interest can refill bucket 1); Bucket 3 = growth equities for 10+ year horizon. The system provided peace of mind without selling into the down market.

title: 3 Shocking Retirement Spending Surprises from 280,000 Retirees
summary: Dave Zoller summarizes JP Morgan's research on 280,000+ retirees: (1) retirement spending decreases over time, peaking in late 40s/early 50s — actual inflation is 1.65%/yr not 3%, suggesting savings needs may be 20-25% lower than typical models; (2) over half of households don't retire all at once and partial retirees spend ~$5K more than fully retired; (3) 60% of retirees experience major spending fluctuations in the first few years — only 18% are 'steady Eddie'.

title: No Tax On Social Security Income?
summary: Dave Zoller clarifies the new 2025 tax bill's 'no tax on Social Security' provision: it's actually a new $6,000-per-person deduction for those 65+, not an exemption of Social Security income. Available 2025-2028 only. Phases out at $75K AGI single / $150K married, gone at $175K / $250K. Available whether you itemize or take the standard deduction. Warning: don't change Social Security claiming strategy based on this new deduction alone.

title: Get More From Your 401K - Mega Backdoor Roth Explained
summary: Dave Zoller explains the Mega Backdoor Roth strategy: most people max their 401(k) at $23,500 ($31,000 for 50+), but the real total contribution limit is ~$70,000. The gap can be filled with after-tax contributions converted immediately to Roth. Example: Sarah ($31K personal + $10K match + $29K after-tax converted to Roth) over 20 years at 7% adds $1.35M+ in tax-free retirement savings.

title: Building a Legacy That Lasts: Hobby Lobby CEO David Green & Author Bill High
summary: Interview with David Green (founder/CEO of Hobby Lobby) and family consultant Bill Hy on the Wealth Beyond Riches podcast. Hobby Lobby started with a $600 loan, now 1,000+ stores and $7.5B+ revenue. Green gives 50% of profits to ministry, takes only a salary based on his job (no profit distributions).

title: 750 Retirees Give Harsh Advice To Anyone Working Past 60
summary: Dave Zoller cites a New York Life study: 48% of retirees aged 62-70 wish they had retired sooner — by an average of four years. Outlines five psychological traps that keep people working too long: one-more-year mentality, safety illusion of a paycheck, identity crisis, success trap (high achievers), and health reality gap.

title: Sell These 5 Things BEFORE You Retire
summary: Dave Zoller lists five things to consider selling or letting go of in retirement: (1) the oversized empty-nest house — client freed $200K equity and cut $15K/yr expenses; (2) over-supporting adult children at expense of retirement security; (3) expensive toys (Tom's classic-car story — kept one of five, freed $180K and $9K/yr); (4) the second car; (5) the work identity itself. Closes with a three-question framework and a 90-day test to evaluate any potential sale.

title: When Retirement Can Hurt Your Marriage
summary: Dave Zoller warns that divorce rates over 50 have doubled since the 1990s — and tripled for those 65+ — even as divorce among younger adults has declined. Three retirement relationship traps: (1) the business owner taking management style into the household (Paul example); (2) being together 24/7 destroys individual identity (Ron Blue: 'I married you for better or for worse, but not for lunch'); (3) loss of professional identity drives one spouse to take over the.

title: Leading from the Inside Out: All Things Together with Heath Hardesty
summary: Interview with Heath Hardesty — pastor, author, former plumber's apprentice — on the Wealth Beyond Riches podcast. Discusses his book All Things Together (out October 14) on apprenticeship as a metaphor for human formation. Topics: imagination as the organ of integration, the disenchanted/dissociated/discontextualized/dislocated modern condition, the practice of joyful generosity, the Proverbs 22:9 bountiful-eye principle, Sabbath rhythms, and why generosity shapes.

title: What Retirement Net Worth Puts You In The Top 1%
summary: Dave Zoller breaks down retirement net-worth percentiles using Survey of Consumer Finances data: median household income for 65-69 ~$50K/yr; top 25% net worth = $395K to $1.16M (include home equity); top 10% ~$2.9M; top 1% ~$21.7M. But 86% of seniors with $50-100K total savings report being comfortable (Federal Reserve data).

title: The Currency of Influence: Why Generosity Wins in Leadership with Brad Lomenick
summary: Interview with Brad Lomenick — strategic advisor to CEOs, leader of leaders, former Catalyst leader — on the Wealth Beyond Riches podcast. Topics: connector vs networker (long-game generosity vs transactional), three connector skills (curiosity, encouragement, pulling gold), Pebb Jackson's model of becoming a student of others, calling vs assignment (river vs the vessel on it), four quadrants for vocational discernment (wiring, passion, God's story,.

title: 75-Year-Olds Share Their Biggest Regret at 55
summary: Dave Zoller argues that the most common regret of successful 75-year-olds isn't 'I wish I'd saved more' — it's 'I wish I hadn't waited so long to live' or 'I wish I'd retired sooner.' Frames retirement as freedom from money-driven time control, not as stopping work. Three insights: the sustainability trap (stress capacity declines with age — burnout is the real cost); experiences compound like investments in a different currency; a real retirement plan is a.

title: Generosity Is the Cure for Fear With Liz Fay
summary: Interview with Liz Fay — Tim's first-ever financial planning client (since 2009) and a former 19-year high school teacher — on her decision to leave teaching for an open-ended Sabbath season. Topics: her early-career generosity (hosting three friends rent-free over the years), fighting scarcity with generosity, the moment in August 2024 she realized she didn't want to continue teaching, role exit and identity, walking a non-defined path, the practice of Sabbath as.

title: Retirement is Short (Spend It Wisely)
summary: Dave Zoller frames retirement as ~1000 weeks left (only ~500 healthy 'go-go' weeks) and shares ~20 lessons grouped into money, relationships, creativity, health, mental models, and life design. Themes: money as time freedom; experiences over objects; energy management over time management; rent before buying; the 90-second emotion rule; the reverse bucket list; test-don't-just-plan retirement design.

title: What Really Happens When You Retire?
summary: Dave Zoller walks through a 72-page retirement study highlighting five mismatches between expectation and reality: 56% retire sooner than planned (only 17% by choice); workers expect to retire at 67 but actual retirees retired at 62; 41% stop working immediately vs the 44% who imagine a gradual transition; median household income drops 33% from working to retirement; 31% claim Social Security at age 62.

title: Purpose Is Greater Than Profit
summary: Long-form Wealth Unriches interview between Dave Zoller and Riley Fuller, founder of nonprofit Humanity and Hope United and Generous Coffee. Fuller recounts a seven-generation Indiana food-business family, a derailment into addiction and arrests, a derivatives-trading career, a 2008 night-of-success crisis that re-surfaced a 2007 Honduras vision, and the founding of a hope-based development nonprofit.

title: 8 Retirement Habits That Lead To Happiness
summary: Dave Zoller summarizes the 90-year Harvard Study of Adult Development (published by George Vaillant, taught by Arthur Brooks) and lists eight research-backed retirement habits. Three are 'fulfillment' habits (active coping, keep learning, prioritize love relationships); four are 'foundational' (don't smoke, careful with substances, healthy weight, daily movement).

title: Don't Retire If This Is You - 5 Warning Signs
summary: Dave Zoller lists five warning signs that someone shouldn't retire yet even if they're financially ready. Three are non-financial: your job currently delivers the three Cs (connection, contribution, creativity); retiring from the business rather than retiring in it; retiring from something rather than to something. Two are financial: not knowing your monthly spending within a reasonable range, and carrying credit-card or auto-loan debt into retirement.

title: Most Retirees Don't Plan For This (And It Costs Them)
summary: Dave Zoller introduces the 'You Inc.' frame: instead of retirement, treat the next chapter as 'rehirement' as CEO of the most important company you'll ever run — yourself. Cites Dr. Peter Attia on three healthspan components (physical, cognitive, emotional) and recommends maximizing healthspan across three departments: physical operations (strength, walks, balance), cognitive development (learning, social connection, sleep), and executive wellness (mission.

title: The Worst Retirement Advice People Actually Follow
summary: Dave Zoller compares two early-2020 retirees: Jane and Kevin. Both had solid plans before COVID hit. Kevin followed seemingly-prudent 'go to cash and wait for clarity' advice, sold during the March 2020 crash, and never got back in. Jane used a three-bucket strategy (seven years of conservative buffer), then executed tax-loss harvesting, Roth conversions during the dip, and rebalancing into stocks at lower prices.

title: Retirement Feels Better When You Do This
summary: Dave Zoller introduces the 'small joy principle' — once retirement quiets the big milestones, small everyday moments (long lines, traffic, dropped Wi-Fi) start to dominate emotional life. If small annoyances can ruin a day, small joys can make one. Recommends a nightly 3-small-joys list to train attention, build gratitude, and recover the feeling that life is full.

title: Retirement Lies vs. Reality
summary: Dave Zoller names four lies retirees commonly tell themselves: (1) 'enough money will make retirement great' — money matters but doesn't fix identity loss; (2) 'retirement is my time, no responsibilities' — aging parents and adult children often need help; (3) 'I won't worry anymore' — worry just changes shape; (4) 'switching from saving to spending will be easy' — decades of saving habits make spending feel terrifying.

title: The “Reverse Bucket List” Changed How I See Retirement
summary: Dave Zoller uses the Walter Mitty snow-leopard scene to introduce Arthur Brooks' 'reverse bucket list' concept. Traditional bucket lists can lower happiness by reframing retirement as a shopping list of unchecked experiences. Reverse bucket list: write down 10 things you've already accomplished, survived, or contributed to. The 'anti-bucket list' celebrates simple everyday pleasures already available.

title: Many People Will Overpay in Retirement Taxes
summary: Dave Zoller explains how married couples can withdraw over $120K/year (~$80K for singles) in retirement and pay zero federal tax by stacking the 0% long-term capital gains bracket on top of the standard deduction. Strategy requires holding investments over a year in taxable brokerage accounts (focused on low-dividend growth investments), funded aggressively during working years alongside normal 401(k) saving. Window: typically pre-Social-Security, pre-RMD retirement years.

title: The Hidden Cost of Retirement Nobody Warns You About
summary: Dave Zoller argues the biggest hidden cost of retirement is loss of meaningful relationships, especially for men, and the year before retirement is the best time to set new ones up. Men form friendships shoulder-to-shoulder through shared activity (men's sheds); women form them face-to-face through direct conversation.

title: The Retirement Mistake Many Regret
summary: Dave Zoller shares Mike, a surgeon who bought a $170K dream car 3 months into retirement and only drove it once a week — should have rented it. Uses the case to introduce Arthur Brooks' satisfaction equation (satisfaction = what you have / what you want) and argues the 'want less' strategy beats the 'have more' strategy because hedonic adaptation always pushes new wants. Suggests two purchase questions: am I buying this for who I am now or who I used to be?

title: These Determine Your Retirement Success
summary: Dave Zoller lists seven factors that separate the happiest retirees: (1) three stages of life and the magical fourth-triangle window where time-money-health all balance; (2) protect 'recovery time' as the third type of time alongside career and relationships; (3) learn to say no after 50; (4) buy back time as your scarcest asset; (5) stop the status game; (6) plan for higher early-retirement expenses; (7) plan for purpose via the three Cs (connection, creativity, contribution).

title: Why People Get Richer After Retirement
summary: Dave Zoller argues it's common for retiree net worth to grow rather than shrink, walking through six drivers: (1) taxes often drop materially (much less than working-years take-home math); (2) expenses generally decline despite the go-go-years bump (no commute, paid mortgage, kids gone, fewer conveniences); (3) healthcare is most expensive pre-65 — Medicare drops premiums dramatically (one couple went from $2K to ~$400/month); (4) Social Security covers most basic.

title: 5 Things That Disappear After You Retire
summary: Dave Zoller walks through five things that quietly disappear from retirees' lives: (1) imposed structure, replaced by self-built routines; (2) work-colleague friendships fade due to lost proximity and shared interest; (3) work ambition needs to be redirected to new challenges or hobbies; (4) urgency and pressure dissolve into a slower pace; (5) work identity goes silent — the hardest one, especially for doctors and executives.

title: Everything Changes at $2M in Retirement Savings
summary: Dave Zoller addresses the 'millionaire paradox' — feeling anxious about spending at $2M+. Walks through three shifts: (1) from accumulation to optimization (tax location, withdrawal sequencing, Roth conversions, RMD management); (2) from rules-of-thumb (4%, target dates) to dynamic guardrails and trigger-based rebalancing; (3) from scarcity thinking to legacy thinking.

title: Once They Learn THIS, They Stop Working
summary: Dave Zoller argues 59½ — not 65 — is the magic retirement age many people overlook. At 59½, the 10% early-withdrawal penalty on tax-deferred accounts disappears, unlocking access without traditional 'enough' standards. Cites William Bengen's 4% rule as built like an aerospace engineer's rocket ship, noting recent research shows the average safely-workable rate was much higher and only 12 of 115 rolling 30-year periods since the 1870s failed.

title: 10 Things Successful Retirees STOPPED Doing (And You Should Too)
summary: Dave Zoller lists ten reasons people miss out on the best retirement: clinging to 'used to be' work identity (Dr. Paul Miller story), underestimating health as a plan input, confusing realistic with pessimistic, keeping options open without choosing, trying to control everything, stopping learning, neglecting post-work relationships, falling into analysis paralysis (Patricia's three-year research detour), staying in the wrong environment, and acting like there's no.

title: The ROTH Lie Most People Believe
summary: Dave Zoller pushes back on the standard advice to do massive Roth conversions early in retirement. Real example: a doctor whose previous advisor recommended a 100% $1M 401k-to-Roth conversion in 2010 — paying at the highest bracket when systematic 8-10 year conversions filling lower brackets first would have saved tens of thousands.

title: The Retirement Sweet Spot: Stop Contributing & Coast
summary: Dave Zoller introduces two retirement milestones that shift the psychology of saving: (1) the crossover point — where annual portfolio growth exceeds annual contributions; (2) the coast number — the amount invested today that, with compound growth alone, hits the retirement income goal without future contributions.

Crawford argues the world is short compute, that demand for AI tokens is exponential rather than cyclical, and that comparisons to dot-com or solar bubbles are misplaced. She discusses specific holdings including Nebius, QXO, and AppLovin, and expresses skepticism about enterprise SaaS surviving the agentic AI transition.
